title: RA.1019 OK Williams
tracklist:
Drum Machine Technicians - Silicone Cream Dreams
Arthur Oskan - Equinoxx
Drivetrain - People!
Davide Squillace - Appendix B
DKS - Deeper Down (We're Going)
Richy Ahmed - Come With Us
DJ Gilb-r - Pressure
Antwon Faulkner - The Dub
Carl Cox - The Exchange 
Throb - Grasshopper 
Loop Kill - Voodoo Slut
Davide Squillace - Appendix A
Petrae Foy & Pete Stanley - What Is It!
TAFKAMP - First Rush 
Bryan Zentz - D-Clash
Star You Star Me - Sweet Things (Ian Pooley Dub)

translation.content: DJing is a craft. It reveals itself over time rather than all at once, and few embody that better than OK Williams. Active since 2019, the London DJ has built a reputation through steady accumulation rather than acceleration, becoming one of the city's most trusted and widely admired selectors in the process. Williams has never been interested in narrowing her taste into a single calling card, and it's no coincidence that her roots lie at NTS Radio. She learned to DJ at the Hackney station, starting out as a volunteer producer in 2017. Musically, she moves easily between moods—sometimes cheeky, sometimes deep—and tempos, playing everything from 160 BPM jungle and amapiano to baile funk, deep house and dub techno. During the pandemic, she also took on NTS's Early Bird slot, guiding the morning listeners with a broader palette that stretched into R&B, ambient, soul and jazz. The throughline isn't genre so much as attitude. There's a clear reverence for dance music's history and its lessons, tempered by a scepticism toward po-faced snobbery or nostalgia for its own sake. That balance often reveals itself in Williams' sets' most memorable moments: peaks built not through obvious escalation, but well-timed curveballs like Britney Spears' "Gimme More" or Mylo's "Drop The Pressure," choices driven less by irony than clarity. As she put it simply when speaking to dance music podcast No Tags last year, "If I like something, I'll play it." Sadly, there are no pop songs on RA.1019. Instead, Williams offers up her "purist" side. Despite her background in radio, she doesn't share mixes often and RA.1019 arrives as a relatively rare studio document. Recorded at home on vinyl, the 90-minute session mostly leans into '90s and early '00s techno and prog house. Featuring deft, flick-of-the-wrist transitions, there's a strong emphasis on groove, loopy rhythms and big, warm basslines. "Ten years ago I tried learning how to play [vinyl] on a friend's 1210s and gave up after a couple of weeks,"Williams tells us below. "Those same decks were then gifted to me years later and were used to record this mix. It's quite nice to see how far I've come." In returning to vinyl, and to the decks that once marked a rocky start, Williams' RA Mix arrives as a fitting marker for the year's end. As 2026 approaches and new resolutions begin to crowd the horizon, RA.1019 serves as a welcome reminder that the best things seldom arrive in a hurry. -Bella Aquilina What have you been up to recently? A lot of touring, radio and digging. A little bit of spiralling here and there. Some reading. How and where was the mix recorded? And can you tell us the idea behind it? It took me a while to figure out what to do for this. It's been a long time since I recorded for a series. It's not something I do often. I really didn't know what to do so I decided to record an all-vinyl mix. Deep in my purist bag, mostly techno which I don't do often. I thought it would be fun to keep it simple. That and I wanted to record at home and I only have a vinyl setup. Ten years ago I tried learning on a friend's 1210s at uni and gave up after a couple of weeks. I was like, "OK I guess that dream is dead." Then a couple of years later I gave it another go and here we are. Those same decks were then gifted to me years later and were used to record this mix. It's quite nice to see how far I've come. What are you looking forward to in the near future? Just improving my practice. Deepening my knowledge of music. The end of social media. Finally organising my record collection. What's one social or political cause you want the world to pay more attention to? I'm very aware that most of the music in this mix is produced by men—a lot of the records are from the '90s and '00s so a sign of the times, but there is still a huge gender imbalance when it comes to music production. I think we should all think about why that is and how we can share knowledge and resources to address that imbalance. What's one club or party that had a major impact on you as an artist? Not so much a specific club or party but I would say being from London and my Nigerian upbringing has had a big influence. I grew up with a lot of different music in the house and this is how I like to play today. House techno, jungle, UK funky, drum and bass, electro, sometimes some amapiano and gqom, batida and kuduro, jersey club… the list goes on. The entire spectrum of Black music inspires me and I think my upbringing definitely programmed me to play it all.
translation.blurb: We close out 2025 with a rare studio mix from the gifted London DJ.
